pattern: Because any value greater than one always admits a remainder of one when divided by itself minus one, the board fills in with every value below the start down to two, and simulating that small, bounded process to a fixed point gives the exact count.
hints: For any number x greater than one, dividing x by (x - 1) always leaves a remainder of exactly one, so x - 1 is always reachable from x.
Chaining that fact means starting from n you can eventually place every integer from 2 up to n on the board; the number 1 itself is never producible this way.
Simulate the growth of the board until nothing new can be added (it stabilizes quickly since every value stays between 1 and n) and count the distinct values collected.

edge_inputs.description: the smallest value greater than one
just below the largest allowed starting value
a starting value with several divisor relationships among 1..n
a starting value one below a power of two

stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    hi = max(1, min(size, 100))
    n = rng.randint(1, hi)
    return [n]
